stories.author: Judd Legum
stories.title: A nuclear deal for the Saudis. A media blackout for Kushner’s corruption.
stories.text: The Saudi government has been aggressively seeking such a deal for years , eager to gain an edge over regional rivals. The agreement lacks safeguards that previous administrations, and many independent foreign policy analysts, viewed as essential to prevent nuclear proliferation. The deal comes after the Saudi government’s Public Investment Fund (PIF) invested $2 billion in Jared Kushner’s private equity fund, Affinity Partners. Since 2021, Kushner, President Trump’s son-in-law, has collected over $110 million from the Saudi government in management fees. In March, Kushner was seeking billions more from the Saudis . (After Kushner’s fundraising was exposed, his efforts were purportedly put on hold.) Kushner also has a close personal relationship with Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman (MBS) — the pair are known to communicate regularly over WhatsApp. In 2021, when the PIF committee originally recommended rejecting Kushner’s proposal due to the high price and Kushner’s inexperience, MBS overruled the recommendation. As he continues to collect fees from the Saudi government, Kushner has resumed his role as a top foreign policy aide to Trump. He has played a key role in virtually

stories.author: Bellingcat
stories.title: Open source tools can help track every stage of a wildfire, from the first reports through to assessing the da...
stories.text: Open source tools can help track every stage of a wildfire, from the first reports through to assessing the damage once the flames are out. Throughout July, the Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service helped researchers monitor how wildfire smoke spread across Canada and the US.

stories.author: Ken Klippenstein
stories.title: The “Greatest Threat” to America?
stories.text: Hegseth testifying to Congress this week Pete Hegseth in his wisdom has identified “the greatest threat that our nation faces,” as he told Congress on Tuesday. It’s not nuclear weapons. Nor is it China. It’s not Iran, Russia, or North Korea. It’s not Cuba or any other country rising or falling. It’s not drugs. It’s not terrorism. It’s not Islam. It’s not “Antifa” or the political left or immigration or migration or anything relating to people. It’s not cybersecurity or the smart phone, artificial intelligence, killer robots, or weapons. It’s not anything related to health or, in Hegseth’s arsenal, laziness or appearance. It’s not an asteroid or some other extinction event. It’s not overpopulation, a pandemic, nor biotechnology in nature. It’s not climate change (of course). And it’s not war itself. In his eternal quest to remind everyone there’s a dumber official than Sebastian Gorka (somehow), the Secretary of War testified under oath that the greatest threat to America is not giving the Pentagon more money . “Not funding this department at $1.5 trillion, I believe, is the greatest threat that our nation faces,” he told the Senate. The one-and-a-half trillion dollar figure refers

stories.author: The Lever team
stories.title: Trump’s Consumer Watchdog Pick Worked For Companies Sued By The Agency
stories.text: President Donald Trump ’s pick to take over the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau , Brian Johnson, previously consulted for a litany of companies and industry groups sued for alleged consumer protection violations, including by the financial regulatory agency he’s now poised to lead, according to financial disclosures reviewed by The Lever . Among Johnson’s clients were payday lenders charging up to 360 percent interest rates, credit reporting agencies that distributed inaccurate information about consumers’ financial status, and a home equity company sued for allegedly trapping homeowners in debt with false promises. If he’s confirmed, Johnson has pledged to recuse himself from matters involving his most recent employer, for which he’s already faced public scrutiny — but not for any of his former clients. The Senate Banking Committee will hold a confirmation hearing on Thursday for Johnson’s nomination, which Trump announced in June. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) began operations 15 years ago this week, in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis, with a mandate to protect the public from the kinds of rogue practices that Johnson’s clients have been accused

stories.author: Drop Site team
stories.title: Israel Firebombed a Family Home Where Children Were Sleeping in Latest Killing Spree
stories.text: The Al-Masri family home in Gaza City after it was bombed by the Israeli military, killing a couple and their four children as they slept on July 21, 2026. Story by Abdel Qader Sabbah, Mohamed Ahmed, Sharif Abdel Kouddous, and Jawa Ahmad GAZA CITY—The charred shell of the Al-Masri family home in the Sabra neighborhood of Gaza City was still smoldering on Tuesday afternoon, hours after the Israel military bombed it, killing Firas and Salsabeel al-Masri and their four children—Faryal, Salma, Amira and Naim—as they slept. The airstrike came without warning in the middle of the night, destroying the upper floor of the simple two-story structure and setting it ablaze. “We were asleep in the middle of the night, at around 2 a.m. or so, when we heard a huge explosion,” Abu Youssef Al-Masri, the children’s grandfather, told Drop Site as he stood in front of the partially collapsed home. “We realized the house had been struck. The house collapsed and caught fire.” The fire spared nothing inside, family belongings burnt to ash lay among broken cinder blocks, the walls that remained standing blackened with soot. “The house burned completely. It was my brother’s home—he, his wife, and their fo

stories.author: Matt Taibbi
stories.title: Don't Let Activists or Robots Choose Your Words
stories.text: The 2026 AP stylebook I just received in the mail checks in at nearly 500 pages, ten times as long as the 1951 edition , 200 pages longer than the 1977 guide . The meaning of “style” has expanded massively over the years. Once, the most important part of the book was a “libel guide,” but that’s long gone, replaced by new sections on “inclusive storytelling,” along with enhanced guidance on “race-related coverage,” immigration, AI, and other politically charged topics. Much of AP style is still good old usage — discreet means prudent, circumspect, while discrete means detached, separate , etc. — but in the 2026 guide, you’ll find a ton of basic descriptive terms shoved into the “avoid” column for questionable reasons, from populist to typical to community to euthansia (but dying with dignity or medical assistance in dying might be okay). Writers are advised not to use a lot of excellent words like “insane” and “demented” and “crazy” outside of quotes, on the grounds of being “derogatory,” while declaring taboo harmless idioms like “turn a blind eye” and “fall on deaf ears.” Meanwhile, a new “inclusive storytelling” section tells editors not to write about diversity and inclusivity —

stories.author: The Lever team
stories.title: Midterm Madness: Defeat in the Desert
stories.text: 🗳️ Welcome to Midterm Madness , the only newsletter you need to stay up to date on 2026 election news. Key race updates, money trails, numbers that cut through the spin, and campaign moments that say more than official talking points ever will — from The Lever team to your inbox, every Wednesday. The Lever ’s work is made possible by our readers. Upgrade Now For Access STORIES TO GO In a hurry? These are the key election stories that crossed our desks this week. 💰 The DNC is on the defensive as Republicans outraise Democrats by more than $100 million and President Donald Trump’s MAGA, Inc. super PAC reports a $400 million war chest. 🏃‍♀️ Sen. Darlene Graham (R-S.C.), who was picked, with Trump’s blessing, to temporarily replace her late brother Lindsey, is running to succeed him for a full six-year term . 😬 New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill (D) revealed yesterday that the state removed more than 6,000 noncitizens erroneously placed on voter rolls following a software malfunction. stories.author: Drop Site team
stories.title: Trump threatens Iran’s bridges and power plants over Hormuz; U.S. and Saudi agree to nuclear deal; RSF drone kills 21 in North Darfur
stories.text: U.S. Wednesday, July 22, 2026 – Renewed U.S. campaign against Iran enters its 12th day. Iran launches wave of retaliatory attacks. President Donald Trump threatens to bomb a civilian target for every attack on shipping in Hormuz. Trump says U.S. will strike Pickaxe Mountain “pretty soon” despite no evidence of nuclear enrichment. Kuwaitis are rationing electricity. U.S. Department of Energy claims that its petroleum reserve can run far lower than previously estimated. International Energy Agency warns of tightening fuel markets. Pakistan requests $10 billion fund for role in Iran negotiations. Iran executes man convicted of links to “anti-revolutionary terrorist group.” Israeli forces continue incursions and airstrikes in southern Lebanon despite “ceasefire” framework. Lebanese PM hails Israeli withdrawal as beginning, Parliamentary Speaker says it “barely qualifies.” Trump promises to “help” Lebanon in meeting with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun, offers no concrete policy. Israel continues to bombard Gaza. Gaza sees rise in miscarriages, Health Ministry warns of cancer drug shortages. Israeli arrests, attacks and seizure of land in West Bank, Jerusalem. Israeli government official

stories.author: Matt Taibbi
stories.title: On Substack's New AI-Spotting Tool, and the Importance of Human Thought
stories.text: Substack CEO Chris Best published an article yesterday called, “ Against Claudefishing ,” in which he announced a new site tool to help editors and users spot potential AI-generated text: We’re partnering with Pangram , the leading AI-detection tool. You’ll be able to scan notes, replies, comments, and posts to see an estimate of how much of the text was written by hand or with AI assistance. The company’s business model was always a bet on the appeal of human voice over corporate concoction, which makes the gathering tidal wave of AI-generated content (as Best notes, it constitutes as much as 40% of some platforms already, with roughly one in four social media posts AI-generated) a threat to their bottom line. Substack sites like Racket are “based on trust between people,” but that trust collapses if writers start using machine helpers to feed the content monkey. I’m going to be on a panel discussion about the issue with other Substack users at 5:00 PM ET tonight. Look at this space, on Notes, or at my Twitter account later for details. A related article is coming out here later.
